extendedContent.content:
The Cornucopia, Latin for "horn of plenty" (cornu copiae), is one of the most enduring symbols
                    of abundance and prosperity in Western culture. This magical goat's horn possesses the divine
                    power to produce unlimited food, drink, and wealth for its possessor, overflowing eternally
                    with the bounty of nature.
                
                
                    Unlike limited treasures that can be spent or consumed, the Cornucopia represents inexhaustible
                    divine generosity. Whatever is taken from the horn is immediately replaced, making it the
                    ultimate symbol of abundance without scarcity, giving without depletion.
extendedContent.title: Description and Nature

extendedContent.content:
The Goat Amalthea
                
                    The most common origin myth connects the Cornucopia to Amalthea, the goat (or goat-tending
                    nymph) who nursed the infant Zeus when he was hidden from his father Cronus on the island
                    of Crete. The young god accidentally broke off one of Amalthea's horns while playing.
                
                
                    In gratitude for her care, Zeus blessed the broken horn with the power of inexhaustible
                    plenty, so that it would always overflow with whatever food or drink its possessor desired.
                    When Amalthea died, Zeus honored her further by placing her among the stars as Capella,
                    and he used her hide to make his aegis shield.
                

                The River God Achelous
                
                    An alternative myth involves Heracles' battle with the river god Achelous for the hand of
                    Deianira. During their wrestling match, Achelous transformed into a bull, but Heracles
                    seized one of his horns and tore it off. The river god exchanged this horn for the
                    original Cornucopia (or in some versions, nymphs filled the broken horn with flowers
                    and fruits, creating the Cornucopia).
                

                
                    "The Naiads took it up and filled it with fruits and fragrant flowers, and hallowed it,
                    and now the good Abundantia is enriched with my horn."
                    — Ovid, Metamorphoses
extendedContent.title: Origin Mythology

extendedContent.content:
Roman Religious Use
                
                    The Romans adopted the Cornucopia as a primary attribute of several deities and personifications.
                    Fortuna (Luck) and Abundantia (Plenty) were frequently depicted holding the horn, as were
                    Ceres (goddess of harvest), Plutus (god of wealth), and various local fertility spirits.
                

                Modern Symbolism
                
                    The Cornucopia remains a powerful symbol today, appearing on coats of arms, state seals
                    (including the Great Seal of North Carolina), corporate logos, and most famously as the
                    centerpiece of Thanksgiving celebrations in North America, symbolizing gratitude for
                    abundance and harvest blessings.
extendedContent.title: Symbolism and Cultural Significance

name: Cornucopia
shortDescription: The Horn of Plenty, Source of Infinite Abundance
symbolism:

sources.name: Pseudo-Apollodorus, Bibliotheca
sources.description: A comprehensive compendium of Greek myths and heroic legends, providing accounts of various mythological figures and events.
sources.title: Pseudo-Apollodorus, Bibliotheca

sources.name: Ovid, Metamorphoses
sources.description: A Roman epic poem recounting a vast array of mythological stories involving transformations.
sources.title: Ovid, Metamorphoses

sources.name: Homeric Hymns
sources.description: A collection of anonymous ancient Greek hymns dedicated to various gods and goddesses, offering insights into their attributes and roles.
sources.title: Homeric Hymns

sources.name: Aristophanes, Plutus
sources.description: A comedy by Aristophanes that satirizes social and economic inequalities, featuring the god Ploutos and his role in distributing wealth.
sources.title: Aristophanes, Plutus

sources.name: Smith, William, ed. A Dictionary of Greek and Roman Antiquities. London: John Murray, 1875.
sources.description: A comprehensive reference work providing detailed information on various aspects of ancient Greek and Roman culture, including mythology, art, and social customs.
sources.title: Smith, William, ed. A Dictionary of Greek and Roman Antiquities. London: John Murray, 1875.

longDescription:
The Horn of Plenty, Source of Infinite Abundance The Cornucopia, Latin for "horn of plenty" (cornu copiae), is one of the most enduring symbols
                    of abundance and prosperity in Western culture. This magical goat's horn possesses the divine
                    power to produce unlimited food, drink, and wealth for its possessor, overflowing eternally
                    with the bounty of nature.
description: The Horn of Plenty, Source of Infinite Abundance

keyMyths.title: The Goat Amalthea and Infant Zeus
keyMyths.description: When Zeus was hidden from his father Cronus in Crete, he was nursed by the nymph Amalthea, who fed him with the milk of a goat. In gratitude, Zeus broke off one of the goat's horns and granted it the power to provide unending nourishment and abundance.

keyMyths.title: Heracles and Achelous
keyMyths.description: In a wrestling match with the river god Achelous for the hand of Deianira, Heracles broke off one of Achelous' horns, which nymphs then filled with fruits and flowers, transforming it into the Cornucopia. This symbolizes Heracles' victory and the taming of nature for the benefit of mankind.

keyMyths.title: Fortuna and Abundance
keyMyths.description: The Roman goddess Fortuna, representing luck and prosperity, is often depicted carrying a Cornucopia, symbolizing her ability to bestow wealth and good fortune upon mortals. This association reinforced the Cornucopia's status as a symbol of bounty and divine favor.
keyMyths.source: Various Roman depictions of Fortuna on coins and statues.

keyMyths.title: Demeter and Agriculture
keyMyths.description: The Cornucopia is sometimes associated with Demeter, the Greek goddess of agriculture, representing the abundance of the harvest and the fertility of the earth. Its overflowing contents signify the earth's capacity to provide sustenance for all.
keyMyths.source: Homeric Hymn to Demeter, various lines referencing agricultural abundance

keyMyths.title: Ploutos and Wealth
keyMyths.description: Ploutos, the Greek god of wealth, is frequently depicted holding a Cornucopia, emphasizing his role as the distributor of riches and material prosperity. This representation firmly established the Cornucopia as a visual symbol of economic abundance and good fortune.
keyMyths.source: Aristophanes, Plutus, various scenes depicting Ploutos and wealth
